preFix.title: Carved lime broth ladle
preFix.subtitle: Green-wood spoon-carving with a wide shallow bowl
preFix.excerpt: A lime-wood broth ladle carved from a green billet. The wide shallow bowl suits thick soups and broths; lime carves cleanly and dries food-safe pale. A first ladle project after the eating spoon.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the axe-to-knife rough-shaping progression; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen-ladle form reference.

content.text: A broth ladle carries more liquid than an eating spoon and stays shallower than a full soup ladle. The wide, shallow bowl suits broths, porridges, and sauces.
Green wood

lime carves cleanly along and across the grain with a sloyd knife; the finished piece dries food-safe pale with no tannin staining. This project sits one step up from a first eating spoon because the handle needs to be slightly longer and the bowl hollowing wider.
Wood
Lime (basswood) is the recommended species. It carves cleanly green or seasoned, holds fine detail, and dries without checking when walls are kept even. A freshly riven lime
billet

28 cm long and 7 cm across is enough material for a ladle with a 16 cm handle and an 11 cm bowl section. Source from coppice off-cuts or a tree surgeon's limings; lime coppices freely and is common in mixed woodland. Birch is a close alternative: similarly clean to carve green, slightly harder when dry.
Tools
Carving axe: for roughing the billet to a spoon profile. The curved bit lets you dish slightly into the bowl area on the rough pass.
Sloyd knife: for refining the handle, the back of the bowl, and the transition from handle to bowl throat.
Hook knife, large: for hollowing the bowl. The wide radius suits the shallow, broad-bowl form.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes of cutting. A lime-loaded strop will develop a pale residue; that is normal.
Raw linseed oil: the food-safe finish. Two-week cure before food contact.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Workspace

Sit on a low carving stool or a garden chair. The billet needs to be at knee height so your arms can work above it without hunching. Good raking light from one side shows the surface clearly as the tool cuts; harsh overhead lighting flattens the texture and hides the grain direction.
Roughing with the axe

Split the billet in half along the pith with a froe, then remove the pith side from one half. You are working with a quarter-section, flat face up. Axe the top face to the spoon profile in plan: round the bowl end, taper the handle. Work with push cuts, the axe travelling away from the body off the end of the billet. The blank should now resemble a spoon in plan but is still full thickness.
Turn the
blank
on its side and remove the excess depth from the handle. The handle should be roughly 12 mm thick when done with the axe. Leave the bowl area at full thickness for now; the axe cannot hollow safely.
Refining with the sloyd knife
The
sloyd knife

takes the handle from rough to round. Use push cuts along the handle length. Hold the work in the four-finger grip, thumb on top. The blade should travel from the shoulder of the handle toward the tip, with the cut ending in open air past the end of the handle. Rotate the handle a quarter-turn between cuts to keep the shaping even.

At the throat where the handle meets the bowl, use thumb-pivot cuts to define the transition. The blade is pivoted by your off-hand thumb pressing on the spine, making short controlled cuts into the throat. This is one of the points where beginners go too deep and weaken the joint; keep the throat at least 8 mm thick on this ladle.

Shape the back of the bowl with push cuts from the rim toward the throat. The grain in lime runs through the bowl cleanly from tip to handle; you are cutting with the grain on the back of the bowl and can use relatively long strokes.
Hollowing with the hook knife

The large hook knife is pulled across the bowl, cutting on the inside radius. Hold the blank so the bowl faces up; your hook-knife hand pulls toward the body while your other hand holds the blank steady. This is a controlled pull cut, not a slash: the forearm does the work, shoulder stays still.

Work from the rim toward the centre in overlapping arcs. Lime hollows quickly because the grain is short and fine; on a broth-ladle bowl 2 mm of wall thickness is the target (slightly thicker than a teaspoon). Test wall thickness by pinching gently. When the wall begins to feel slightly springy under pressure, stop removing material.
Strop the
strop
every ten to fifteen minutes. A hook knife that drags rather than slices has lost its edge and is unsafe to push through.
Finishing

Leave the ladle to air-dry at room temperature for two to three days before oiling. Rapid drying in sun or near a radiator will check the thin bowl walls. Once the surface has dried to a matt chalky appearance (the lime will lighten noticeably), apply raw linseed oil liberally with a cloth. Let it soak for one hour, then wipe off all surplus. Repeat after 24 hours. Let the piece cure for two weeks before putting it to food contact.
Variations
Deeper bowl: Use a hook-knife-medium instead of large and work the centre deeper for a porridge ladle. Increase wall thickness to 3 mm for the deeper form.
Longer handle: Extend the handle to 22 cm for a jam-pan or preserving ladle. Reinforce the throat to 10 mm minimum.
Left-handed version: Reverse the hook-knife direction so the bowl hollow tilts to the left rather than the right.
Care
Hand-wash only; never dishwasher and never long soak. Dry promptly with a clean towel. Re-oil every two or three months with a wipe of raw linseed, or when the wood starts to look dry and pale.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Work the hook knife from both sides of the bowl, not just toward the centre from one edge. Alternate directions every two or three strokes.

In lime the grain runs end-to-end through the spoon. On the back of the bowl, cuts from the rim toward the throat go with the grain. Reversed cuts will lift and tear. Stop, flip the direction, and re-cut.
attrs.symptom: The knife tears rather than slices

attrs.fix: Keep the throat 8 mm minimum and dry slowly at room temperature. If a small crack appears, press a little raw linseed in with a fingertip; it will stabilise as the oil cures.
attrs.cause: Throat left too thin or dried too fast

attrs.fix: Wipe briskly with a dry cloth, then leave in a warm dry room for another week. Raw linseed is slow-curing; a thin coat left without wiping can take four weeks to cure fully.
attrs.symptom: The finished ladle smells of linseed even after two weeks

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
